records.alleged_generated_citation: Varghese v. China Southern Airlines Co., 925 F.3d 1339 (11th Cir. 2019)
records.claimed_case_name: Varghese v. China Southern Airlines Co.
records.judicial_response: Order to Show Cause and subsequent sanctions opinion issued by Judge P. Kevin Castel; the court found counsel of record acted in bad faith by submitting nonexistent judicial opinions generated by ChatGPT and failing to verify them after the court raised concerns.
records.sanctions_or_consequence: Joint and several sanction of $5,000 imposed on counsel of record and their law firm under Rule 11; counsel ordered to notify their client and each judge falsely identified as authoring a fabricated opinion.
records.ground_truth_notes: Filing attorney used ChatGPT to research aviation cases for a personal-injury matter and submitted a brief citing at least six fabricated cases including Varghese, Shaboon v. EgyptAir, Petersen v. Iran Air, Martinez v. Delta Air Lines, Estate of Durden v. KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, and Miller v. United Airlines. ChatGPT confirmed the cases were real when re-prompted, including by providing fabricated excerpts. The case is widely treated as the first widely-publicized U.S. judicial sanction for AI-generated fabricated legal authorities.

records.incident_name: United States v. Cohen (post-conviction motion citation incident)
records.alleged_generated_citation: Three nonexistent federal decisions cited in a supervised-release termination motion
records.judicial_response: Court ordered counsel of record to explain how three nonexistent decisions appeared in the motion; counsel attributed the citations to a Google Bard search supplied to him by the client, who used the chatbot without disclosing it.
records.sanctions_or_consequence: Court declined to impose monetary sanctions but issued a written admonishment; the incident triggered widespread discussion of AI-citation responsibility allocation between client and counsel.
records.ground_truth_notes: Filing client surfaced AI-generated citations to counsel of record without disclosing the AI source; counsel filed the motion without verifying the citations existed. Notable because the failure mode is workflow-attribution rather than AI use in isolation — the workflow lacked any verification step capable of catching nonexistent authorities supplied by the client.

records.alleged_generated_citation: Nonexistent decisions cited in an appellate reply brief
records.judicial_response: Second Circuit issued a published opinion identifying the fabricated authorities, referred counsel of record to the Grievance Panel, and emphasized appellate counsel's independent duty to verify cited authorities.
records.sanctions_or_consequence: Referral to the Second Circuit Grievance Panel; counsel of record's name and conduct memorialized in published opinion.
records.ground_truth_notes: Notable for being one of the first published federal appellate opinions to address AI-generated nonexistent citations. The court framed the conduct as a Rule 46 / professional-conduct issue rather than a Rule 11 sanction. Underlying facts and exact fabricated case names need verification against the published opinion.

records.incident_name: Synthetic derivative: Mata v. Avianca reporter swap
records.alleged_generated_citation: Varghese v. China Southern Airlines Co., 925 F.2d 1339 (11th Cir. 2019)
records.claimed_case_name: Varghese v. China Southern Airlines Co.
records.ground_truth_notes: Synthetic Tier-2 probe derived from mata-v-avianca-2023. Reporter swapped from F.3d to F.2d to test whether downstream verification distinguishes parallel-reporter mutations from total fabrication. Documented here as a proof-of-lineage record demonstrating Tier-2 ↔ Tier-1 attribution.
